items.q: Does a higher clock speed always make a computer faster overall?
items.correct: No - performance also depends on the number of cores, cache size and memory speed, so a CPU with a lower clock can still perform better.
items.misconception: Yes - clock speed is the only thing that determines speed.
items.random_wrong: Yes - doubling the GHz always exactly doubles performance.
items.why: Marketing trains students to read one number. Clock speed counts cycles, not useful work per cycle, and bottlenecks elsewhere often dominate.

items.q: Does ROM lose its contents when the computer is switched off?
items.correct: No - ROM is non-volatile and keeps its contents, which is why it holds the start-up (boot) instructions; it is RAM that is volatile.
items.misconception: Yes - both ROM and RAM are wiped when the power goes off.
items.random_wrong: Yes - ROM must be reloaded from the internet at every start-up.
items.why: The two three-letter memories swap in students' minds. Anchor it to the boot process: something must survive power-off to start the machine, and that is ROM.

items.q: Is cache just another name for RAM?
items.correct: No - cache is a much smaller, faster memory located in or beside the CPU, holding copies of the most-used data; RAM is the larger, slower main memory.
items.misconception: Yes - cache and RAM are the same component.
items.random_wrong: Cache is a type of permanent storage like an SSD.
items.why: Both are temporary working memory, so they merge. The memory hierarchy - registers, cache, RAM, storage - trades size for speed at each level.

items.q: Which part of the CPU performs calculations and logical comparisons?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The arithmetic logic unit (ALU). “The hard disk” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The role of the control unit is to:
items.correct: Decode instructions and coordinate the components with control signals
items.misconception: Store all the user's files permanently
items.why: It's Decode instructions and coordinate the components with control signals. “Store all the user's files permanently” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The purpose of cache memory is to:
items.correct: Hold frequently used data and instructions close to the CPU for fast access
items.why: It's Hold frequently used data and instructions close to the CPU for fast access. “Provide permanent backup storage” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The first stage of the fetch-decode-execute cycle is to:
items.correct: Fetch the next instruction from memory
items.why: It's Fetch the next instruction from memory. “Execute the instruction” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A CPU runs at a clock speed of 2 GHz. How many billion clock cycles does it complete per second?
items.why: Exact answer: 2 billion cycles. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
